sentences.text: That shows you-- I believe that I mentioned to you this-- how low the characters of people have been, of some of the high ranking police, Jewish police, went.
And-- and if I-- I have to mention that to you.
[SIGHS]
The other thing [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was before my escape to the partisans, to go to the partisans.
I have my wife to-- on the Aryan side to see that she would be safe.
The other thing, talking about the police, the Jewish police-- happens that every few hours, as told you, that the block are-- block was on four-- what are call it?
Guard towers.
Guard-- guard towers.
And when they were-- could see that already coming, a guard is coming, to change them.
So that was a possibility for my wife and one of the-- and the wife of the Mr.
Riszfeld, who went also to the partisans with me together, to open the door.
There was a little opening where the-- to open them.
And when they were going, they went-- when they could see that they are going already to change them, and so on, so they went down.
And there was only a strip there-- there-- to let them out.
So the police didn't want to open the door.
And one of them tell me, if he want-- if Kaufman wants to save his wife, let them pay.
And that time, I paid them 70 ruble in gold.
They were gold--
Coins.
And let out my wife that time.
That was the second time how the police, the Jewish police, were handling and were dealing.
Who-- who did Ala escape with-- escape with?
Who was with her?
That was the wife.
Lutka.
Lutka Riszfeld
L-U-T-K-A R-I-S-Z-F-E-L-D.
She knows about this, that I will be by the partisans.
She knows this, that I was by-- by the partisans.
And everyone only was helping for the best.
From Kailis?
You are talking about the times of Kailis?
Well, you had to have been-- [SIGHS] have been people with whom I was also conferring a lot-- after work, being.
That was one.
He was in-- also in the underground.
Aronovich, ?] his name was.
And we called him [?
Aronic. ?] And he was by the partisans too.
He escape.
He escape, even earlier than I escape.
He was by the partisans-- [?
Aronovich. ?]
I was still working in the tannery.
And we were always-- when we met and we were talking over, even to make something under the earth to get out the young people to run away to the forest, and so on, to-- to go to the partisans.
I was [INAUDIBLE].
That was only a very, very-- because next, on the other side, Lithuanians were living, [INAUDIBLE].
I was very, very careful.
Very-- no, [SIGHING] not--
But dangerous, very dangerous.
Yeah.
No.
No.
I have to come back a little bit.
As I mentioned, that the first murders, our murders, they were the arm band-- Lithuanians.
Every one who what hasn't got a arbeitschein were arrested to the Lukiskes, what I call the-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Were collected.
And every day, every second day, 250, 300 people [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were finishing them off-- everyone what hasn't got a arbeitschein.
After a few days-- two, three days-- they asked not only the arbeitschein from the place where he's working, the person, but has to be also a knowledge by the Feldkommandantur.
There was no gestapo.
There was no other institutions.
Only the Feldkommandantur was there.
And I noticed that, every few days, people were-- were going to-- to-- to-- to finish.
And they-- they-- they were finished off in that-- in [?
Punari. ?] And so I started in how to save and how to get people-- and people, of course, the Jews were hiding in the attics, and the cellars, and that all.
And they were looking for them, you know, to-- people what are not working, and whatever.
They haven't got the schein, the arbeitscheins, that they have been also with the-- acknowledged by the Feldkommandantur.
So I went to that Martin Hanze, and I told him, listen, I haven't got chemicals.
I haven't got extracts.
And we have to build some covers, because we have to bring the-- from the trees, the-- what do you call this?
The [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH].
Bark.
The bark, yeah.
The bark from that.
And this-- now, it's winter time.
It will rain.
And that has to be dry, and so on.
So we have to build-- so-- and I would have to build this.
He said to me, oh, the best idea would be if we will take some people-- you've got plenty people in the tanneries working.
I said to him, Mr.
Hanze, that is impossible, because the people are filling out them's obligation, them's work.
And so-- so-- so he asked me where-- where-- where [INAUDIBLE]?
I said I-- don't leave it to me.
I've already arranged some people, Jews, that they will come and they will build this for us.
Well, all right.
So I start to run from one house to the other house, calling out to give me the names, and so on, that I could make for them their arbeitscheins, and so on.
In the beginning-- When-- when was this?
What-- what--
That was in the beginnings, right away, in the beginning.
I forgot to tell you.
In-- not in the ghetto.
That was in the beginning, when the war start.
I told you, that-- that-- that the arm-- the white armbands, Lithuanians, they were our killers.
So-- and they gave me-- I assembled about 52 of people, what gave me the names.
Not then, but they were hiding.
They were frightened for my-- myself too.
So the wives-- they were only arresting men at that time.
And they gave me the names.
I came back, I told him, all right.
And he wrote this on a type machine.
And after, only the names on that copy, a few [INAUDIBLE].
So I said to him, no.
All right, that this stamp of that [NON-ENGLISH], of that engineer, that-- that normally, that was mine, the director from that.
So to get the [NON-ENGLISH].
So I told him this, and that will not be enough.
I went, I got the stamp on the old scheins.
And I came back to him, I told him, listen, that will not be enough.
That has to be the Polish-- the Lithuanians, they're asking for this, the acknowledgement of that-- the stamp of the Feldkommandantur.
Ah, he didn't want to talk to me.
He said that is enough.
That's enough.
So I took off mine coat.
I took off mine-- what I call it?
The David star from Birkenau, and through the whole city went to the Feldkommandantur.
As a Jew, just imagine what-- I wasn't thinking, completely thinking.
That was just that I'd done it.
I done it.
I came there.
That was a [SPEAKING GERMAN] Came up there.
There was a cabinet, and glass, and one [NON-ENGLISH]-- a-- a-- a General, sitting, looking on the papers, or some-- looking there.
And I knocked to the door, just opposite him.
And I knocked at the door.
I didn't open the door.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] That is just to disappear.
And I risked already to go through the whole city.
And risking so, and to go back, not to have the stamp from the Feldkommandantur?
I was around, around, around, around.
It was a colossal cabinet there.
And behind him, was another door.
It was very hard to find out.
There was an opening there?
Or was not an opening?
But I find another door.
And so I didn't knock to the door.
I went to him.
I sat next to him to-- there, on the left side.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Where-- where have you [INAUDIBLE]?
Where-- so I was only just putting.
And he was not looking on me, and just his stamp was going on.
And so I-- I-- I arranged an [NON-ENGLISH] This mean a camp of Jews what they haven't got the arbeitscheins.
That was-- we called them that time, [? lebenschein, ?] a schein to leave.
And so-- and they were working all the time [INAUDIBLE].
Names?
Yes.
Boruchuovicz, Nelkin-- a daughter is here-- lives here.
The father's name?
Oh gosh, name-- no.
Forgot.
But the-- Mrs.
Gross' ?] father-- [?
Gross, ?] [?
Gross-- ?] She's a-- she's a [?
Gross ?] wife.
The father was amongst them too.
No.
No.
It was a German, a general, a-- a high ranking-- a feldkommandant.
I didn't show it to him.
I-- I-- I-- I took the whole people, as an [NON-ENGLISH], as a-- as a part of-- [INAUDIBLE].
Do you know if-- or how many, or if any of these people survived the war?
Was one, [?
Butkoff, ?] but he passed away here.
The-- the father was-- was alive.
He was living in Israel.
And he was killed from a-- he felled off from a truck, and killed himself.
Her father-- yes, where-- yes.
I would like to go back a little bit, because not mentioning a name with whom we-- I was in contact in-- and [?
Hereshek ?] Riszfeld was in contact, not to mention the name of-- and not to talk about this would be not right.
The family Riszfeld got a little son, just a newborn son.
Before the action of Jewish children, till 13 years, he was asleep from the Kailis.
And to a family on the name Ludwig Fronckiewicz.
He was Commandant [NON-ENGLISH] from the [NON-ENGLISH] I'll say it in Polish, maybe it will be easier.
Yeah?
[SPEAKING POLISH].
And his name was Fronckiewicz.
Could you spell his name, please?
F-R-O-N-- Fron-- N-C-K-E-K-A-E-W-I-C-Z.
And that child was in that flat.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The daughter-- they got one daughter.
She was the only one daughter and working as a secretary for the treuhander in the furrier factory.
A treuhander?
That Is the-- the-- every factory has to have a-- a person what work and is responsible for the work what is the factory was doing.
Of course, that was a German.
Most of them, they were Germans, you know? a volksdeutche.
And that child, still alive now.
Is living in-- is not a-- is not a child anymore.
Is living in America.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And-- [POINTS] [SIGHS] I was in contact with them, because, on the factories where I was working, was also the underground.
The Polish underground was there.
So that-- that looks to me that the contact what I was having with them, that was through the place where I was working.
And I was knowing what the underground is doing.
And they pulled me in also to work for them, you know?
To describe his flat, that was a-- was an arsenal, actually.
Every few days, bought or stealed all equipment for the partisans.
Was under the--
The floorboards.
Between the floorboards, that was-- that was our arsenal, formally, there.
That was not in Kailis.
That-- that was [LAUGHS] not far from the-- where the block is.
That was in the flat-- the flat where that Fronckiewicz, with his wife and the daughter, was living there.
And that boy thinks the daughter of that-- Fronckiewicz' daughter, was taken and kept in that flat.
My escape?
From Kailis.
The last day, when I was in the factory carrying on my business, one of the underground people came to me and gave me his [GERMAN].
That was like a passport, with his photograph.
His name was Kazimiez Zdinofwski.
[CHUCKLES] K-A-Z-I-M-I-E-Z-- Kazimiez.
Z-D-I-N-O-F-W-S-K-I.
Zdinofwski.
He told me, tomorrow, I don't want to-- we don't want to see you here.
You have to go to the partisans.
I came to-- I didn't went straight, going back.
I went to Fronckiewicz' place.
I gave him that.
He said, thank you, very much.
Now, I need a photo, your photo.
He took off the photo from that-- his passport.
And he put in mine and added that part of the stamp, added on mine photo.
And so, I was already not Moses Kaufman, but Kazimiez Zdinofwski.
I got a photo.
I got-- he made the photo.
Equipment to make a photo.
It was-- no.
That was next to it.
I-- that was next to the-- or he was-- what it was they're called?
[NON-ENGLISH] I will- I will-- that-- maybe that will come to me what-- what the name of the street was.
That was not far from that--
So the next thing was to go to the-- to the partisans.
He arranged already and gave what they're calling this [NON-ENGLISH]?
That is parole?
Parole?
You could say parole, yes?
And that I and that Riszik Riszfeld, the father of that son, are going to the partisans.
And we came up there, and with that parole, of course.
And that commandant from that [NON-ENGLISH]-- from the-- from the partisans there, knows about this already, that we are coming.
I escape.
I escaped.
During-- that was from the-- from-- from the factory, I already-- when I got this, I was already illegally-- illegal in the-- in the-- in the-- in the-- in Kailis.
I was only not existing.
And there was-- there-- there were already selections done there, too.
In Kailis.
That's correct.
No.
The tannery?
No.
Who was guard-- guarding me?
Guarding, was only the German-- the people, what they have, with Hanze-- with Martin Hanze on top.
With mine identities.
With mine.
That was mine.
I don't remember quite rightly what kind of a identity he got.
But we got the parole-- the parole, yes, we have just called it?
No-- password.
What's that?
Password is better?
Password.
And we came to that Szczerbic.
Yeah.
That was the name-- Szczerbic.
It's a help of a Polish from the-- outside from the high ranking-- and the kings, and so on.
I beg you?
Szczerbic.
S-Z-C-Z-E-R-B-I-C.
That was the northern part of [NON-ENGLISH] [LAUGHS] The northern part of-- that's in-- close to one of the towns-- [NON-ENGLISH].
By going with that identity, my identity, through and out-- out from the town.
And so--
Oh, that take only just what is about-- six hours?
Five hours?
Six hours.
During the day already.
I-- I was an Aryan.
Thank you.
